docs.title: How to use this site
docs.text:
1. Pick a learning path under Learning Paths for a guided sequence.
  2. Deep-dive topics in Computer Science, Databases, Networking, and related sections.
  3. Practice using collapsible interview questions and linked problem sets.
  4. Bookmark Resources for books, blogs, courses, and channels worth returning to.

Start locally

Run mkdocs serve from the repository root and edit Markdown under docs/—the site reloads as you save.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Embeddings map text (or images) into dense vectors so semantic similarity approximates distance in vector space. They power search, clustering, classification, and retrieval for RAG.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Keyword search misses paraphrases; embeddings capture meaning well enough for nearest-neighbor retrieval at scale.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Training approaches include contrastive learning and dual encoders. Operational concerns: normalization, dimensionality, domain mismatch, staleness as models update, and batching for throughput.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   Text[Text] --> Enc[Encoder]   Enc --> Vec[Vector]   Vec --> ANN[Approximate NN index]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Metric choice Cosine similarity on L2-normalized vectors is common; verify metric matches your index library defaults.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Python — cosine similarity 
import math

def cosine(a: list[float], b: list[float]) -> float:
    dot = sum(x * y for x, y in zip(a, b))
    na = math.sqrt(sum(x * x for x in a))
    nb = math.sqrt(sum(y * y for y in b))
    return dot / (na * nb) if na and nb else 0.0

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
Why re-embed documents when upgrading models?

Vector spaces are not comparable across unrelated models—plan migrations and dual-write/dual-query strategies.

What is Matryoshka embedding?

Training embeddings where prefixes of the vector remain informative—enables flexible storage tiers.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Evaluate embedding quality with a small labeled relevance set
  • Compare bi-encoder retrieval vs cross-encoder reranking for rerank latency

docs.text:

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: AI systems engineering applies models—especially large language models (LLMs)—inside products with retrieval, tooling, evaluation, and safety guardrails. This section connects model basics to production patterns.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Shipping AI features requires more than prompt snippets: you need data pipelines, evaluation loops, latency/cost controls, and operational monitoring.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Progress through LLM basics, Embeddings, Vector databases, RAG architecture, Prompt engineering, and Production LLM systems.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   User --> App[Application]   App --> LLM[LLM API]   App --> Vec[(Vector DB)]   Vec --> Emb[Embeddings]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Evaluation first Define tasks, golden sets, and metrics before scaling complexity—otherwise you cannot tell if changes help.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Text — minimal RAG loop 
query -> embed -> retrieve top-k -> augment prompt -> generate -> cite sources

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
What is the biggest risk of naive RAG?

Retrieved noise or stale documents can override model priors—needs ranking, filtering, and evaluation on answer faithfulness.

How do you control cost for LLM features?

Cache embeddings, batch requests, choose smaller models for easy tasks, truncate context, and monitor tokens per request.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Build a tiny RAG over your notes with citations
  • Create a rubric to grade hallucination rate on a fixed test set

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Hugging Face Course
  • OpenAI Cookbook

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Large language models predict the next token given prior context, trained on broad text corpora with self-supervised objectives. Inference samples or greedily picks tokens subject to temperature, top-k, and top-p.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: LLMs generalize across tasks via prompting and fine-tuning, reducing bespoke model training for many product features.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Concepts: tokenization, context windows, parameters vs inference compute, fine-tuning vs RLHF/DPO, hallucinations, calibration, latency trade-offs across model sizes.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart TB   Prompt[Prompt tokens] --> Model[Transformer LM]   Model --> Next[Next token distribution]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Context limits Long prompts increase cost and latency; retrieval and summarization compress relevant information into the window.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Python — OpenAI API sketch 
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()
resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4.1-mini",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain TCP slow start in 3 bullets."}],
    temperature=0.2,
)
print(resp.choices[0].message.content)

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
What does temperature do?

Scales softmax logits—higher temperature increases randomness; lower makes outputs more deterministic.

Why do models hallucinate?

They optimize for plausible continuation, not grounded truth—unless constrained by retrieval, tools, or fine-tuning.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Compare latency and quality for small vs large models on your task
  • Token-budget a prompt that includes system, tools, and user content

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Attention Is All You Need
  • Stanford CS324 — LLMs

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Production LLM systems add SLOs, observability, cost controls, safety, rollouts, and governance around models. Reliability matters as much as prompt quality.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Prototypes break under load, bad outputs harm users, and spend can spiral without metering—engineering closes those gaps.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Operate routing across models, caching for repeated prompts, streaming responses, rate limits, quotas, fallbacks, evaluation in CI, red teaming, logging with PII controls, A/B tests, and human review queues.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   Client --> GW[Gateway]   GW --> Route[Router]   Route --> M1[Small model]   Route --> M2[Large model]   GW --> Obs[Metrics/Tracing]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Human in the loop For high-risk domains, automatic generation is a draft—require review or confidence thresholds before actions with side effects.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Text — SLO examples 
95% of chat completions < 800ms p99 excluding model time
Error rate < 0.1% on gateway
Cost per successful task < $0.02

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
What should you log for LLM calls?

Request IDs, model version, latency, token usage, safety flags, and retrieval ids—not raw PII unless necessary and compliant.

How do you roll out a new prompt safely?

Shadow mode, canary traffic, offline evals, and automated regression checks on golden prompts.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Add tracing from API gateway to vector retrieval to model call
  • Design a budget alert on daily token spend per tenant

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Google SRE — monitoring
  • NIST AI RMF

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Prompt engineering shapes model behavior with instructions, formats, examples, and constraints—without weight updates. It is the fastest iteration loop for product behavior.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Models are general; prompts specialize them. Clear structure reduces ambiguity and improves reliability for extraction, classification, and tools.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Patterns: system vs user messages, few-shot examples, chain-of-thought where appropriate, JSON schema outputs, tool calling schemas, defense against injection (treat untrusted text as data).

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   Sys[System instructions] --> LLM[LLM]   User[User input] --> LLM   LLM --> Out[Structured output]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Prompt injection Never trust user content to be passive data—separate instructions from data, filter, and sandbox tools.

docs.title: Code Examples
Interview Questions
docs.text:
When are few-shot examples better than zero-shot?

When the desired format is niche or constraints are subtle—examples demonstrate the pattern faster than long prose.

How do you reduce variance across runs?

Lower temperature, constrain outputs, use schemas, and add self-consistency checks for critical tasks.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Write a prompt that extracts structured resume fields into JSON
  • Harden a support bot prompt against prompt injection attempts

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• OpenAI prompt engineering guide
  • Anthropic prompt library

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) combines retrieval from a knowledge base with generation from an LLM, grounding answers in citations and reducing hallucinations when retrieval works well.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Models alone may lack private or up-to-date data; RAG injects relevant context per query without full fine-tunes for every fact.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Pipeline: ingest chunking, embed, index; query embed, retrieve top-k, rerank optionally, prompt with citations, generate answer. Add guardrails, evals, and feedback loops.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart TB   Docs[Documents] --> Chunk[Chunking]   Chunk --> Emb[Embed]   Emb --> Store[(Vector store)]   Q[User Query] --> QE[Embed query]   QE --> Store   Store --> Ctx[Context]   Ctx --> LLM[LLM]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Chunking strategy Too small loses context; too large dilutes relevance—experiment with headings, sliding windows, and structure-aware splits.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Text — prompt with citations requirement 
Answer using ONLY the provided passages. If insufficient, say you do not know.
Passages:
[1] ...
[2] ...
Question: ...

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
What is a common failure mode in RAG?

Retrieved chunks are irrelevant or contradictory; the model confidently merges them—needs better retrieval, reranking, or constraints.

How do you handle document updates?

Version chunks, re-embed changed sections, invalidate stale answers in UI, and track source timestamps.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Add cross-encoder reranking to a baseline bi-encoder pipeline
  • Measure faithfulness with a labeled dataset of (question, context, answer)

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• LangChain RAG
  • LlamaIndex

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Vector databases store embeddings and support approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search at scale with filters (metadata), replication, and operational tooling.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Brute-force kNN is slow for millions of vectors; ANN indexes (HNSW, IVF, PQ) trade recall for speed.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Compare managed services (Pinecone, Weaviate Cloud, etc.) vs self-hosted (Milvus, Qdrant, pgvector). Understand index types, recall/latency trade-offs, hybrid search (keyword + vector), and multi-tenant isolation.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   Ingest[Embeddings] --> Index[ANN index]   Query[Query vector] --> Index   Index --> Hits[Top-k results]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Filters first Pre-filter metadata before ANN when selectivity is high—otherwise vector search wastes work on irrelevant partitions.

docs.title: Code Examples
Interview Questions
docs.text:
What is HNSW?

Hierarchical navigable small world graph for approximate nearest neighbors—fast queries with good recall on many workloads.

How do you update embeddings incrementally?

Upsert vectors with new versions, run background reindex jobs, and monitor recall after index parameter changes.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Benchmark recall@k vs latency for your dataset and index settings
  • Design hybrid search combining BM25 and vector scores

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• FAISS documentation
  • pgvector

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Scaling APIs means increasing sustainable throughput under latency SLOs. Levers include horizontal scaling, efficient data access, caching, async processing, and backpressure.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Traffic grows; incidents happen during peaks. Scalability work connects application design to infrastructure limits and cost.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Techniques: stateless app servers, connection pooling, read scaling, partitioning, rate limiting, bulkheads, auto-scaling policies, load testing, and capacity planning. Pair with System design — scalability.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart TB   LB[Load balancer] --> A1[App]   LB --> A2[App]   A1 --> C[(Cache)]   A2 --> C   A1 --> DB[(DB primary)]   DB --> R[(Replicas)]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Latency percentiles matter Optimizing p99 often matters more than mean latency; trace tail events caused by GC, cold caches, or slow queries.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Go — server timeouts (illustrative) 
srv := &http.Server{
  Addr:              ":8080",
  ReadHeaderTimeout: 5 * time.Second,
  WriteTimeout:      10 * time.Second,
  IdleTimeout:       60 * time.Second,
}

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
What is backpressure?

A mechanism to signal producers to slow down when consumers cannot keep up—essential to avoid unbounded queues and OOMs.

How does connection pooling help?

Reuses expensive TCP/TLS and DB handshakes; unbounded pools can exhaust DB resources—tune max connections per instance.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Propose scaling plan for 10× traffic on a read-heavy catalog API
  • Identify bottlenecks in a flame graph showing DB time dominating requests

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Google SRE — load balancing
  • AWS Well-Architected — performance

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Authentication establishes who is calling; authorization decides what they may do. Web APIs commonly use sessions, API keys, and tokens (often JWTs) with OAuth2/OIDC for delegated login.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Trust boundaries fail without strong identity. Password handling, token lifetimes, rotation, and revocation separate toy demos from production systems.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Study password hashing (Argon2/bcrypt), MFA, OAuth2 flows (authorization code with PKCE for SPAs), JWT structure (claims, signature, audience), refresh tokens, revocation lists, and service-to-service credentials (mTLS, workload identity).

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: sequenceDiagram   participant U as User   participant C as Client   participant IdP as Identity provider   participant API as API   U->>C: Login   C->>IdP: Authorization code + PKCE   IdP-->>C: Tokens   C->>API: Access token   API-->>C: Protected resource

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: JWTs are not a session store They are signed blobs; if you need instant revocation or complex session state, pair tokens with server-side session tracking or short lifetimes + refresh.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Pseudocode — verify JWT claims 
verify_signature(token, jwks)
assert iss == expected_issuer
assert aud contains our_api
assert exp > now
check nbf if present

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
What is the difference between OAuth2 and OpenID Connect?

OAuth2 delegates authorization to access resources; OIDC adds identity tokens and standard userinfo on top of OAuth2.

Why use PKCE?

Prevents authorization code interception for public clients by binding the token exchange to a code verifier.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Threat-model storing refresh tokens in mobile secure storage vs browser
  • Compare session cookies vs bearer tokens for same-site vs cross-site APIs

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• OAuth 2.0 Security BCP
  • OWASP Authentication Cheat Sheet

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Background jobs move work off the request path: emails, billing, analytics, image processing, and retries. Queues decouple producers and consumers and absorb bursts.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Users expect fast HTTP responses. Long-running or retry-heavy tasks belong in workers with supervision, idempotency, and dead-letter handling.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Study message queues (SQS, RabbitMQ, Kafka for event streams), at-least-once delivery, idempotent consumers, visibility timeouts, priority queues, scheduled jobs (cron + leader election), and workflow engines for multi-step processes.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   API --> Q[Queue]   Q --> W1[Worker]   Q --> W2[Worker]   W1 --> DB[(DB)]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Exactly-once is elusive Aim for effective exactly-once via idempotent writes and deduplication keys rather than impossible distributed guarantees everywhere.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Pseudocode — idempotent job handler 
on message m:
  if already_processed(m.id): ack and return
  begin txn
    apply side effects
    record m.id as processed
  commit
  ack

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
What is a poison message?

A message that always fails processing; move to a dead-letter queue after retries for manual inspection.

How do you choose between a queue and a stream?

Queues for task distribution with competing consumers; streams/logs for replay, ordering partitions, and event sourcing patterns.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Design retries for a payment webhook consumer calling an unreliable partner API
  • Compare Kafka partitions vs RabbitMQ queues for an analytics pipeline

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• AWS SQS developer guide
  • Celery documentation — Python workers

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Caching stores copies of expensive computations or remote reads closer to consumers—in-process, on servers, reverse proxies, CDNs, or dedicated stores like Redis.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Latency and load on databases dominate costs. Effective caching policies improve user experience until staleness or invalidation bugs appear—design carefully.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Patterns: cache-aside, read-through, write-through, write-behind. Concepts: TTL, eviction, negative caching, singleflight, cache stampede mitigation. For distributed caches, consider consistent hashing and hot key problems.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   API --> Cache[(Redis)]   Cache -. miss .-> DB[(Database)]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Define freshness requirements Every cached object needs an owner for invalidation and an acceptable staleness window tied to user-visible behavior.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Python — cache-aside sketch 
def get_user(user_id: str):
    key = f"user:{user_id}"
    hit = redis.get(key)
    if hit:
        return json.loads(hit)
    row = db.fetch_user(user_id)
    redis.setex(key, 300, json.dumps(row))  # 5m TTL
    return row

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
What is dogpile/stampede and how do you mitigate it?

Many processes recompute on expiry simultaneously—use locks, early refresh, or request coalescing.

When is write-through preferable?

When reads must be strongly consistent with writes and you can tolerate write latency to keep cache always warm.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Choose TTL strategy for configuration vs user profile data
  • Design invalidation for nested aggregates displayed in a dashboard

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Redis documentation
  • Instagram engineering — caching — case studies in the wild

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Backend engineering turns business rules into reliable services: APIs that clients call, data that outlives requests, and operational properties like security, scalability, and observability.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Products depend on servers that compose databases, caches, queues, and third parties. This section aligns language-agnostic concepts you will see in every mature stack.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Topics include REST API design, Authentication, Microservices, Caching, Background jobs, and API scalability.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   Client --> API[API layer]   API --> Auth[AuthZ/AuthN]   API --> DB[(Database)]   API --> Cache[(Cache)]   API --> Q[Queue]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Operational mindset Features ship; incidents teach. Design for logs, metrics, traces, safe deploys, and rollbacks from day one—not as an afterthought.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Text — request lifecycle checklist 
Authenticate -> Authorize -> Validate input -> Execute use case -> Persist -> Emit events -> Respond

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
What is the difference between authentication and authorization?

Authentication establishes identity; authorization decides whether that identity may perform an action on a resource.

Name three ways to scale a read-heavy API.

Caching, read replicas, and materialized views or denormalized read models—each with consistency trade-offs.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Sketch OpenAPI for a resource with pagination and filtering
  • Identify failure modes in a synchronous chain of three internal HTTP calls

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Martin Fowler — microservices
  • Google SRE Book

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Microservices split a system into independently deployable services owned by small teams. Benefits include scaling and isolation; costs include network chatter, distributed transactions, and operational complexity.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Monoliths are simpler early; microservices help when boundaries align with team structure, scaling profiles, or technology diversity—if you can afford the distributed systems tax.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Define bounded contexts, API contracts, service discovery, resilience patterns (timeouts, retries with jitter, bulkheads, circuit breakers), observability, and data ownership (avoid shared databases when possible).

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   GW[Gateway] --> A[Orders]   GW --> B[Users]   A --> Q[Queue]   B --> DB1[(DB)]   A --> DB2[(DB)]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Conway’s Law Systems mirror teams; bad team boundaries become painful service boundaries—fix org alignment before carving services.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
YAML — retry with exponential backoff (conceptual) 
retry:
  max_attempts: 5
  base_delay_ms: 50
  max_delay_ms: 2000
  jitter: full

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
What is a saga?

A sequence of local transactions with compensating actions to achieve a global outcome without a single distributed ACID transaction.

When is a monolith better?

Early product stages with unclear domains, small teams, and low operational maturity—optimize for iteration speed first.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Identify data ownership boundaries for an e-commerce checkout flow
  • Compare synchronous HTTP chains vs events for notifying inventory

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Building Microservices (Sam Newman)
  • AWS — distributed systems patterns

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: REST APIs model resources with HTTP methods and status codes. Good design balances predictable URLs, clear semantics, versioning, and error shapes that clients can program against.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Clients and multiple backend teams coordinate through HTTP contracts. Consistent patterns reduce integration bugs and speed onboarding.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Use nouns for resources, nested routes sparingly, idempotent PUT/DELETE, pagination (cursor preferred for large sets), filtering, consistency in error payloads, and explicit versioning (/v1 or headers).

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   Client --> Gateway[API Gateway]   Gateway --> Svc[Service]   Svc --> DB[(DB)]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: HATEOAS (optional) Hypermedia controls can guide clients, but many teams prefer stable JSON schemas plus OpenAPI documentation for practicality.

docs.title: Code Examples
Interview Questions
docs.text:
When would you use POST instead of PUT for updates?

When updates are partial, non-idempotent, or when the server assigns identifiers—though PATCH can be used for partial updates with explicit semantics.

How do you version APIs without breaking mobile clients?

Maintain compatibility windows, additive changes, feature flags, and optional fields—avoid removing or renaming fields without deprecation cycles.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Write OpenAPI for CRUD on projects with team members as sub-resource
  • Compare cursor vs offset pagination for a high-volume feed

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Microsoft REST API guidelines
  • OpenAPI Initiative

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Employee Stock Option Plans (ESOPs) grant options to buy shares at a strike price, aiming to align employees with company growth. Value depends on exit outcomes, dilution, and exercise costs.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Startups compete for talent with equity; understanding vesting and taxation prevents painful surprises.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Terms: grant size, strike, vesting schedule, cliff, post-termination exercise window, 409A valuations, preferred stock preferences affecting common stock value.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   Grant[Option grant] --> Vest[Vesting]   Vest --> Exercise[Exercise]   Exercise --> Liquidity[Liquidity event]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Illiquidity risk Options may expire worthless; early exercise and tax implications vary—get professional advice for large amounts.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Text — due diligence questions 
- Fully diluted share count and your percent ownership
- Latest 409A and preferred round price
- Exercise window after leaving
- Company repurchase rights and transfer restrictions

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
What is dilution?

New shares issued reduce relative ownership unless you participate pro rata—common in fundraising.

Why do employees early exercise?

Potentially favorable tax treatment in some jurisdictions—depends on rules and company policy.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Model exit scenarios with different multiples and dilution assumptions
  • Compare ISO vs NSO at a high level for your jurisdiction

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Carta equity education
  • Holloway — equity compensation

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Financial literacy for engineers means budgeting with variable equity, understanding taxes at a high level, saving for goals, and avoiding common behavioral traps—not day-trading memes.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Compensation complexity increases with seniority; basic literacy reduces stress and supports long-term decisions.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Topics: budgeting, emergency funds, retirement accounts (locale-specific), diversification, fees, inflation, and risk tolerance. Separate investing education from career decisions.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart TB   Income[Income] --> Tax[Taxes]   Income --> Spend[Spending]   Income --> Save[Saving/Investing]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Personal finance is personal Optimize for sleep-at-night money first—aggressive investing before an emergency fund is fragile.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Text — simple monthly plan skeleton 
Fixed costs: rent, utilities, insurance
Variable: food, travel, hobbies
Savings rate target: X% of net
Retirement: automate contributions

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
Why diversify instead of picking stocks?

Reduces idiosyncratic risk; most individuals lack persistent edge—index funds are a common baseline.

What is lifestyle inflation?

Spending rises with income, eroding savings rate—watch it after promotions.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Track spending for a month and categorize surprises
  • Read your pay stub and list pretax vs posttax deductions

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Bogleheads wiki
  • Khan Academy — personal finance

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Negotiation aligns your offer with market value and personal constraints. The process works best with data, professionalism, and clarity on what you need to sign.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Offers are rarely final on first pass; recruiters expect structured negotiation within published bands.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Research benchmarks, prioritize levers (base vs equity vs signing bonus vs level), communicate in writing, and avoid ultimatums unless you mean them. Time-box decisions respectfully.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   Prep[Research + priorities] --> Ask[Clear ask]   Ask --> Tradeoffs[Tradeoffs]   Tradeoffs --> Close[Written agreement]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Negotiate with the decision-maker loop Recruiters coordinate; hiring managers sometimes approve exceptions—understand who can say yes.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Text — example negotiation email skeleton 
Hi <name>,
I'm excited about <team>. After reviewing the offer and market data for <level/location>,
I was hoping we could explore <specific adjustment>. I’m flexible on structure and can discuss
tradeoffs between base and equity. Could we schedule a short call this week?
Thanks,
<you>

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
Is it okay to disclose competing offers?

Share what you are comfortable with; focus on verifiable facts and avoid fabrications—integrity matters long-term.

What if they say the band is fixed?

Ask about signing bonus, relocation, level reconsideration, or start date flexibility—sometimes bands are softer than stated.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Role-play negotiation with a peer playing recruiter
  • Write three polite counter scenarios with different priorities

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:

  • Fearless Salary Negotiation — book/site

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Engineering compensation mixes base salary, bonuses, equity, and benefits. Structures vary by company stage, geography, and role level—benchmarks are directional, not guarantees.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Understanding components helps you compare offers fairly and plan cash flow, especially when equity is illiquid or volatile.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Public companies often emphasize RSUs; startups use stock options with strike prices and cliffs. Bonuses may be target percentages with performance multipliers. Benefits (401k match, insurance, stipends) matter to total comp.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart TB   TC[Total compensation] --> Base[Base]   TC --> Bonus[Bonus]   TC --> Equity[Equity]   TC --> Ben[Benefits]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Taxes and jurisdiction Equity taxation is complex and locale-specific—consult a qualified professional for large decisions.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Text — offer comparison checklist 
- Base currency and payment cadence
- Bonus: target %, performance criteria, guarantee period
- Equity: type, vesting, cliff, refreshers, dilution risk
- Benefits: health, retirement match, PTO, parental leave
- Level and promotion cadence expectations

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
How do levels map across companies?

Roughly via scope and impact, but titles are not 1:1—use leveling guides and recruiter calibration.

What is a four-year vest with a one-year cliff?

No equity vests until 12 months; then typically monthly—read your grant documents carefully.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Build a spreadsheet modeling two offers with different equity volatility assumptions
  • List questions to ask HR about refresh grants and promotion equity bumps

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Levels.fyi — crowdsourced compensation
  • Glassdoor — qualitative signals

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Indexes accelerate lookups by maintaining auxiliary structures (often B-trees or hash tables) that map key values to row locations. They are the primary tool for turning full scans into selective seeks.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Tables grow; scanning every row for each query does not scale. Indexes trade extra storage and slower writes for faster reads on hot access paths.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Study B-tree/B+tree indexes for range queries, hash indexes for equality, and composite indexes where column order matters. Learn covering indexes (INCLUDE columns) and why selectivity drives usefulness.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart TB   Index[B+Tree index] --> Leaf[Leaf pages]   Leaf --> Heap[Table heap / clustered index]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Leftmost prefix rule For composite `(a, b, c)`, queries filtering only on `b` may not use the index efficiently—column order encodes sortability.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
SQL — composite index 
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_user_created
  ON orders (user_id, created_at DESC);

-- Likely index-friendly
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE user_id = 123
ORDER BY created_at DESC
LIMIT 20;

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
Why can too many indexes hurt write performance?

Each insert/update/delete must maintain every affected index structure, increasing I/O and lock contention.

What is index-only scan?

When all selected columns exist in the index, the engine may avoid visiting the heap/clustered table.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Design indexes for a timeline query with pagination by (user_id, id)
  • Compare sequential scan vs index scan using EXPLAIN on growing data

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Use The Index, Luke
  • CMU 15-445 — indexing lectures

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Databases manage durable, shared state with defined consistency and query semantics. This section connects relational theory to engines, indexes, transactions, and scaling strategies used in production.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Almost every backend persists data. Interviews and design discussions assume you can reason about schemas, isolation, failure modes, and how to scale reads and writes safely.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Progress from relational modeling and SQL (SQL basics, Joins) to physical storage (Indexing), correctness under concurrency (Transactions), performance tuning (Query optimization), and growth patterns (Scaling databases).

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   App[Application] --> Pool[Connection pool]   Pool --> DB[(Database)]   DB --> Storage[(Storage engine)]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Logical vs physical SQL describes a logical result; the planner chooses indexes and join algorithms to approximate that result efficiently.

docs.title: Code Examples
Interview Questions
docs.text:
What problems do databases solve that files on disk do not?

Concurrent access, transactional semantics, indexing for fast lookup, declarative querying, and operational tooling (backups, replication).

When would you choose SQL vs a document store?

Prefer SQL when you need joins, constraints, and mature transactional semantics; consider documents for flexible schema and nested aggregates when access patterns match.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Design a schema for a multi-tenant SaaS with strong tenant isolation
  • Given slow queries, walk through reading an EXPLAIN plan and proposing indexes

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Use The Index, Luke! — practical indexing guide
  • PostgreSQL Documentation — reference implementation details

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Joins combine rows from two or more tables according to predicates. They are central to relational modeling and to query plans that dominate application latency at scale.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Normalized schemas split entities across tables; joins reconstruct the relationships at query time. Understanding join types prevents silent data loss and cartesian explosions.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Know INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL joins and when each preserves non-matching rows. Study equi-joins vs non-equi joins and semi-joins (EXISTS, IN). At execution, planners may choose nested loop, hash join, or merge join.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   T1[Table A] --> Join[Join operator]   T2[Table B] --> Join   Join --> Out[Result]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Cartesian products Missing join conditions or overly broad predicates can explode row counts—watch for accidental cross joins.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
SQL — inner vs left 
-- Inner: only matching pairs
SELECT u.email, o.id
FROM users u
INNER JOIN orders o ON o.user_id = u.id;

-- Left: all users, orders optional
SELECT u.email, o.id
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.user_id = u.id;

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
How does a hash join work?

Build a hash table on the smaller input (often), probe with the larger; efficient for equi-joins when memory allows.

When is nested loop join preferred?

Small inputs, selective indexes, or when one side is tiny—O(n·m) worst case but great with strong selectivity.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Given two tables, write queries for “users without orders” and “orders with user details”
  • Analyze a slow join using EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) in PostgreSQL

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Use The Index, Luke — joins
  • MySQL JOIN optimization — planner concepts transfer broadly

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Query optimization bridges declarative SQL and efficient execution: statistics, cost models, join ordering, and index selection. Profiling slow queries is a core backend skill.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: The same logical query can differ in runtime by orders of magnitude depending on plans and data distribution. Optimization prevents regressions as data grows.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Use EXPLAIN plans, understand sequential scan, index scan, bitmap scans, join order, and sort/hash aggregate. Maintain statistics, manage vacuum/ANALYZE (or equivalents), and watch for N+1 ORM patterns.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   SQL[Query] --> Parse[Parse]   Parse --> Rewrite[Rewrite]   Rewrite --> Plan[Planner]   Plan --> Exec[Executor]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Correlation vs causation in plans High cost in plans points to investigation; confirm with runtime, buffers, and actual row counts versus estimates.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
SQL — hint via rewrite (PostgreSQL style) 
-- Prefer enabling parallel query / adjusting work_mem in session for large sorts
SET LOCAL work_mem = '256MB';
SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount)
FROM orders
WHERE created_at > now() - interval '30 days'
GROUP BY customer_id;

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
What makes a query planner choose a sequential scan?

Large fraction of rows selected, missing indexes, low selectivity, or small tables where setup cost dominates.

How do you diagnose an N+1 query problem?

Observe many similar queries per request in logs; batch with joins or WHERE IN loaders.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Take a slow join and iterate: index → rewrite → denormalize (last resort)
  • Compare plans before/after adding a composite index

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• PostgreSQL EXPLAIN
  • Use The Index, Luke — SQL rewrites

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Scaling databases means increasing read/write throughput and storage without sacrificing correctness goals. Common strategies include replication, partitioning, caching, and choosing specialized engines.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Single-node databases hit vertical limits. Teams shard data, add replicas, and introduce caches—each choice shifts failure modes and consistency guarantees.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Study primary/replica replication, synchronous vs asynchronous, failover, partitioning/sharding keys, hot spots, read-after-write consistency, and global distribution trade-offs. Pair with System design — database scaling.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   App --> Router[Query router]   Router --> Shard1[(Shard 1)]   Router --> Shard2[(Shard 2)]   Shard1 --> Replica1[Replica]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Shard key selection Poor keys create uneven partitions and undo scale benefits; often combine user id with time bucketing for telemetry workloads.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Conceptual — routing pseudocode 
def shard_for_tenant(tenant_id: int, num_shards: int) -> int:
    return hash(tenant_id) % num_shards

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
How does leader-follower replication handle failures?

Promote a replica with bounded data loss depending on sync policy; split-brain risks require quorum/fencing.

What is a thundering herd on cache expiry?

Many requests miss cache simultaneously; mitigate with probabilistic early expiry, singleflight, or request coalescing.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Design a sharding scheme for multi-tenant SaaS with per-tenant isolation
  • Compare read replicas vs caching for a read-heavy profile feed

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Designing Data-Intensive Applications — replication and partitioning
  • AWS Database Blog — operational patterns

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: SQL is the lingua franca of relational databases: you declare schemas with DDL, mutate rows with DML, and query with a declarative SELECT. Fluency in core syntax and set thinking unlocks debugging and modeling discussions.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Backends persist entities and relationships; SQL expresses constraints and queries that ORMs eventually translate. Reading and writing SQL remains essential even when abstractions hide details.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Master SELECT projection, WHERE filtering, GROUP BY aggregation, HAVING on aggregates, ORDER BY sorting, and LIMIT/OFFSET pagination. Understand keys, constraints, and NULL semantics.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart TB   Parser[SQL text] --> Planner[Planner]   Planner --> Executor[Executor]   Executor --> Storage[Storage]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Set-oriented thinking Avoid row-by-row mental models unless necessary; prefer joins and aggregates that let the engine optimize.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
SQL — aggregationSQL — upsert pattern 
SELECT u.id, COUNT(o.id) AS order_count
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.user_id = u.id
GROUP BY u.id
HAVING COUNT(o.id) >= 5
ORDER BY order_count DESC;

INSERT INTO inventory (sku, qty)
VALUES ('ABC', 10)
ON CONFLICT (sku) DO UPDATE
  SET qty = inventory.qty + EXCLUDED.qty;

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING?

WHERE filters rows before grouping; HAVING filters groups after aggregation.

Explain NULL behavior in comparisons.

Comparisons with NULL yield UNKNOWN in three-valued logic; use IS NULL/IS NOT NULL and COALESCE/NULLIF carefully.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• LeetCode Database problems (aggregations and joins)
  • Schema a blog with posts, tags, and comments in 3NF

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• SQLBolt — interactive lessons
  • PostgreSQL SELECT tutorial

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Transactions group operations into atomic units with ACID properties. Isolation levels define which anomalies may appear when transactions run concurrently.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Concurrent backends would corrupt data without clear rules for interleaving reads and writes. Transactions are the contract between application expectations and engine behavior.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Understand BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK, durability via WAL, and isolation levels: Read Uncommitted, Read Committed, Repeatable Read, Serializable. Learn lost update, dirty read, non-repeatable read, phantom read, and how engines mitigate them (MVCC, locking).

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: sequenceDiagram   participant A as Txn A   participant DB as Database   participant B as Txn B   A->>DB: BEGIN   B->>DB: BEGIN   A->>DB: UPDATE row   B->>DB: READ row   Note over A,B: Behavior depends on isolation level   A->>DB: COMMIT

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: MVCC snapshot Many systems implement repeatable reads by showing a transaction a consistent snapshot of rows as of start time, using row versions rather than long read locks.

docs.title: Code Examples
Interview Questions
docs.text:
What is two-phase locking (2PL)?

Growing phase acquires locks; shrinking phase releases—used to achieve serializability in classical theory; practical systems mix with MVCC.

Give an example of a write skew anomaly.

Two transactions read overlapping sets of rows and make decisions based on counts that become invalid after both commit—may require serializable isolation or explicit constraints.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Implement idempotent payment processing with unique idempotency keys
  • Explain how your database’s default isolation maps to anomalies you have seen in logs

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• PostgreSQL isolation
  • Designing Data-Intensive Applications — transactions

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Arrays store elements in contiguous memory, enabling O(1) index access and predictable cache behavior. Most interview problems manipulate indices, prefixes, or windows over arrays.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Arrays are the default representation for sequences in memory. Understanding indexing, mutability, and copying is prerequisite for strings, matrices, and many dynamic programming states.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Common techniques include prefix sums for range queries, two pointers for sorted arrays, sliding windows for subarray constraints, and in-place rearrangement with careful swap logic.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   A[Array memory] --> B[Index i]   B --> C[Read O(1)]   B --> D[Write O(1)]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Contiguity trade-off Fast random access comes with insertion/deletion costs in the middle—often O(n) due to shifting elements.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Python — prefix sumPython — sliding window (fixed size k) 
def prefix_sums(nums: list[int]) -> list[int]:
    ps = [0] * (len(nums) + 1)
    for i, x in enumerate(nums):
        ps[i + 1] = ps[i] + x
    return ps

def range_sum(ps: list[int], l: int, r: int) -> int:
    return ps[r + 1] - ps[l]

def max_sum_subarray(nums: list[int], k: int) -> int:
    if not nums or k <= 0:
        return 0
    window = sum(nums[:k])
    best = window
    for i in range(k, len(nums)):
        window += nums[i] - nums[i - k]
        best = max(best, window)
    return best

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
Given an array, find the maximum subarray sum (Kadane).

Track the best ending here and the running sum; reset running sum when it drops below zero. Handles negative numbers; empty array edge cases need explicit policy.

How do you rotate an array in-place?

Use triple reverse for rotation by k: reverse whole array, reverse first k, reverse remainder—O(n) time, O(1) extra space.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• LeetCode 53 — Maximum Subarray
  • LeetCode 238 — Product of Array Except Self
  • LeetCode 56 — Merge Intervals

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:

  • CP-Algorithms — Prefix sums

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Dynamic programming (DP) solves optimization and counting problems by memoizing overlapping subproblems or building iterative tables along a clear state order. It is one of the most interview-relevant paradigms after arrays and graphs.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Brute-force recursion often revisits the same states exponentially. DP collapses that redundancy by storing results of subproblems with well-defined dependencies.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Steps: identify state, transition, base cases, and whether the order is top-down (memoization) or bottom-up (tabulation). Common families include knapsack, LCS/LIS, interval DP, grid paths, and digit DP.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   Subproblem[i,j] --> Subproblem[i-1,j]   Subproblem[i,j] --> Subproblem[i,j-1]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Optimal substructure The optimal answer for the whole problem can be built from optimal answers to subproblems—verify the property before applying DP.

docs.title: Code Examples
Interview Questions
docs.text:
What is the difference between greedy and DP?

Greedy makes locally optimal choices without revisiting; DP is needed when local choices conflict and overlapping subproblems require memoization or tabulation.

How do you reduce space for grid DP?

If transitions only need the previous row, keep two rows or even one row sliding over columns.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• LeetCode 322 — Coin Change
  • LeetCode 1143 — Longest Common Subsequence
  • LeetCode 312 — Burst Balloons (interval DP)

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• DP Patterns (leetcode discuss) — community pattern map
  • Algorithms (Erickson) — DP — free book chapters

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Graphs model relationships: vertices and edges, directed or undirected, weighted or unweighted. They are the backbone of networks, dependencies, maps, and state spaces.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Many real systems are graphs—social networks, package dependencies, routing tables. Interview problems often reduce to DFS/BFS, topological sorting, shortest paths, or union-find.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Representations include adjacency lists (common), adjacency matrices (dense graphs), and edge lists. Master BFS for shortest path in unweighted graphs, Dijkstra for non-negative weights, Bellman–Ford when edges can be negative, and topological sort for DAGs.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: graph LR   A((A)) --> B((B))   A --> C((C))   B --> D((D))

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: State space graphs Many “board” or “string transformation” puzzles are implicit graphs—nodes are states, edges are valid moves.

docs.title: Code Examples
Interview Questions
docs.text:
Detect a cycle in a directed graph.

Use DFS with three colors (unvisited, visiting, visited) or Kahn’s algorithm for topological sort attempt.

When is BFS preferred over DFS?

BFS for shortest path in unweighted graphs and level-order; DFS for connectivity, exhaustive paths, or memory-limited stacks.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• LeetCode 200 — Number of Islands
  • LeetCode 207 — Course Schedule
  • LeetCode 743 — Network Delay Time

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• CP-Algorithms — Graphs
  • USACO Guide — Graph Traversal

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Data structures and algorithms (DSA) are the vocabulary of efficient programs: how we store information and the procedures we use to transform it under time and space constraints. This section connects theory to patterns you will reuse in interviews and production systems.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Every non-trivial program picks a representation (arrays, trees, graphs) and a strategy (two pointers, BFS, DP). Naming these patterns accelerates debugging, complexity analysis, and communication with peers.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Study progresses from linear structures (Arrays, Strings, Linked lists) to constrained access (Stacks and queues), hierarchical data (Trees), relational models (Graphs), and optimization over choices (Dynamic programming).

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Placeholder diagram for a typical problem-solving pipeline:

graph LR Problem --> Model Model --> Structure Structure --> Algorithm Algorithm --> Complexity

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Pattern recognition Most interview problems are variations of a small set of templates. The goal is not memorizing every problem, but mapping unknown tasks to known structures and invariants. Complexity discipline State O(·) time and space for your approach before coding; adjust the approach if bounds fail the stated constraints.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Python — complexity mindsetPseudocode — invariant sketch 
def sum_first_n(nums: list[int], n: int) -> int:
    """O(n) time if n ~ len(nums); O(1) extra space."""
    total = 0
    for i in range(min(n, len(nums))):
        total += nums[i]
    return total

function two_pointer_sorted(arr):
  left = 0, right = len(arr) - 1
  while left < right:
    if arr[left] + arr[right] == target: return (left, right)
    elif sum too small: left += 1
    else: right -= 1

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
How do you choose between a hash map and a tree-based map?

Prefer hash maps when you need expected O(1) lookups and either have a good hash for keys or can tolerate rare worst-case behavior. Prefer balanced trees when you need ordered traversal, range queries, or deterministic worst-case performance.

What is amortized analysis and where does it show up?

Amortized analysis averages the cost of a sequence of operations. Classic examples include dynamic array resizing and disjoint-set union optimizations.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Arrays: two-sum variants, sliding window maximum, trapping rain water
  • Graphs: number of islands, course schedule (cycle detection)
  • DP: longest increasing subsequence, coin change, edit distance

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• CLRS — canonical algorithms reference
  • VisuAlgo — interactive visualizations
  • LeetCode — timed practice with discussion threads

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Linked lists chain nodes with pointers. They shine when you need O(1) insertion at a known position and can tolerate O(n) random access or cache-friendly sequential scans on arrays.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Pointer manipulation is a standard interview screen for careful reasoning about edge cases: empty lists, single nodes, and cycles.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Master dummy node patterns, fast/slow pointers for cycle detection and midpoint splits, and list reversal iteratively and recursively.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   N1[Node] --> N2[Node]   N2 --> N3[Node]   N3 --> NIL[null]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Cycle detection Floyd’s algorithm uses two pointers advancing at different speeds; if they meet, a cycle exists.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Python — singly linked list nodePython — reverse linked list (iterative) 
class ListNode:
    def __init__(self, val: int = 0, next: "ListNode | None" = None):
        self.val = val
        self.next = next

def reverse_list(head: ListNode | None) -> ListNode | None:
    prev = None
    cur = head
    while cur:
        nxt = cur.next
        cur.next = prev
        prev = cur
        cur = nxt
    return prev

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
Merge two sorted linked lists.

Walk both pointers, always choosing the smaller head; handle empty lists and tail appends.

Detect a cycle and return the start node.

After fast/slow meet, move one pointer to head and advance both one step at a time; the meeting point is the start.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:

  • LeetCode 141 — Linked List Cycle
  • LeetCode 146 — LRU Cache (often implemented with doubly linked list + map)

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:

  • VisuAlgo — Linked List

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Stacks are LIFO structures; queues are FIFO. Both appear as explicit data structures and as implicit patterns in recursion, breadth-first search, and scheduling.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Many algorithms reduce to “what do we process next?” Stacks model depth-first and nested structure; queues model level-order expansion and fair ordering.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Implementations use arrays (with resizing) or linked lists. Monotonic stacks solve next-greater-element problems; deque supports efficient push/pop on both ends.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart TB   subgraph Stack[LIFO Stack]     S3[top]     S2 --> S3     S1 --> S2   end   subgraph Queue[FIFO Queue]     Q1[front] --> Q2     Q2 --> Q3[back]   end

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: BFS uses a queue Layer-by-layer traversal in trees and graphs typically uses a queue; DFS uses an explicit stack or the call stack.

docs.title: Code Examples
Interview Questions
docs.text:
Implement a queue using two stacks.

Use one stack for enqueue and pop from the other stack when needed; amortized O(1) operations.

What is a monotonic stack used for?

Maintaining increasing/decreasing order to answer “next greater element” style queries in linear time.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:

  • LeetCode 739 — Daily Temperatures (monotonic stack)
  • LeetCode 622 — Design Circular Queue

docs.title: Resources

  • USACO — stacks and queues

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Strings are sequences of characters. In many languages they are immutable; algorithms often convert to lists of characters for in-place edits or use rolling hashes for fast substring comparison.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Text processing powers parsers, search, bioinformatics sketches, and security-sensitive comparisons. String problems test careful indexing and awareness of encoding/locale quirks.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Key ideas include two pointers, rolling hash (Rabin–Karp), tries for prefix sets, and KMP for linear pattern matching. For interviews, KMP is less common than hashing and two-pointer approaches.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: graph LR   Pattern --> Hash   Text --> Window   Window --> Hash   Hash --> Compare

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Immutability In Python, Java, and many managed languages, naive concatenation in loops can be O(n²). Use lists or builders to accumulate.

docs.title: Code Examples
Interview Questions
docs.text:
Implement a function to check if a string is a palindrome ignoring non-alphanumeric characters.

Normalize with two pointers from both ends, skipping invalid characters; compare case-insensitively.

How does a rolling hash help substring search?

Update the hash in O(1) as the window slides, comparing hashes before full string comparison to reduce work.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• LeetCode 125 — Valid Palindrome
  • LeetCode 3 — Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
  • LeetCode 76 — Minimum Window Substring

docs.title: Resources

  • CP-Algorithms — String hashing

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Trees generalize linked lists into hierarchical structure: a root, children, and parent pointers. Binary trees and binary search trees (BSTs) are interview staples; balanced search trees back many standard library collections.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Hierarchical data models filesystems, DOMs, organizational charts, and decision paths. Tree traversals are the foundation for many graph algorithms on restricted topologies.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Know preorder, inorder, postorder traversal (recursive and iterative), BST invariants, height vs depth, and balanced tree concepts (AVL, red-black at high level). For coding, practice lowest common ancestor, serialize/deserialize, and diameter.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: graph LR   Root((Root)) --> L((Left))   Root --> R((Right))

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: BST property Inorder traversal of a BST yields sorted keys—useful for validation and kth smallest queries with augmentation.

docs.title: Code Examples
Interview Questions
docs.text:
Validate a binary search tree.

Propagate valid ranges per node or track inorder predecessor; watch for integer overflow on edge values.

Serialize and deserialize a binary tree.

Use preorder with null markers, or BFS with level-wise encoding; ensure round-trip correctness.
