docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Behavioral interviews assess how you collaborate, handle conflict, prioritize, and learn from mistakes. Strong answers are specific, recent, and grounded in outcomes.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Companies optimize for predictable collaboration at scale; past behavior is the most common proxy they can access in an hour.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Prepare 8–12 stories spanning leadership, conflict, deadlines, mistakes, mentorship, and cross-functional work. Use STAR or CAR (Context, Action, Result). Map stories to common prompts and company values.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   Prompt[Question] --> Story[Chosen story]   Story --> STAR[STAR structure]   STAR --> Followups[Anticipate follow-ups]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Balance humility and confidence Show agency without arrogance; credit teams without erasing your contribution.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Text — Amazon LP style skeleton 
Customer obsession: shortened onboarding from 14 days -> 3 days by ...
Insist on highest standards: caught data drift in evals before launch by ...
Bias for action: shipped behind feature flag in 48h to validate ...

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
Describe a time you missed a deadline.

Explain scope, early warning, stakeholder comms, mitigation, and prevention—ownership matters more than perfection.

Tell me about working with a difficult stakeholder.

Focus on empathy, alignment on goals, communication cadence, and measurable compromise.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Record yourself answering five prompts; trim filler words
  • Peer mock interviews with rotating companies’ value rubrics

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Google re:Work — interview guide
  • STAR method overview

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: A resume deep dive prepares you to defend every line: technologies, scale, trade-offs, and your specific contributions versus the team’s. Interviewers probe until your mental model is clear.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Strong candidates articulate impact with metrics and can zoom from business outcome to architecture to debugging story without hand-waving.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: For each project capture: problem, constraints, your role, architecture diagram, metrics, failure/lesson, what you would improve. Align stories with STAR for behavioral follow-ups.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   Bullet[Resume bullet] --> Story[Structured story]   Story --> Depth[Technical depth]   Depth --> Metrics[Impact metrics]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Claim ownership precisely “We designed” vs “I led” vs “I contributed” matters—honesty builds trust and avoids probing collapses.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Text — project one-pager template 
Project: Payments retry service
Context: 10k USD/day failed retries, customer churn risk
My role: Owner for worker architecture + idempotency keys
Stack: Go, Postgres, SQS, Datadog
Outcome: -42% failed retries in 6 weeks; p99 latency 380ms -> 210ms
Failure: Misconfigured DLQ visibility — postmortem + runbook

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
Tell me about a technical disagreement you had.

Focus on data, user impact, and resolution—avoid blaming; show how you validated assumptions.

How do you discuss a failed project?

Own the outcome, describe early signals missed, concrete corrective actions, and what you monitor now.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Write STAR stories for your top three projects
  • Draw architecture diagrams from memory in 5 minutes each

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Tech Interview Handbook — resume
  • Levels.fyi blog — compensation context while job searching

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Building with LLMs blends classical backend skills with retrieval, evaluation, and safety considerations. This path moves from model basics to production RAG and serving patterns.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: The field changes quickly, but durable concepts—tokenization, embeddings, vector search, prompt contracts, and observability—stay relevant across model generations.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Model literacy — What LLMs optimize and where they fail.
  2. Representation — Embeddings and vector stores as searchable memory.
  3. RAG & prompts — Grounded answers and reliable interfaces.
  4. Production — Latency, cost, guardrails, and operations.

docs.title: Suggested sequence
docs.text: Phase Focus Start here 1 Foundations Introduction, LLM basics 2 Retrieval Embeddings, Vector databases 3 Applications RAG architecture, Prompt engineering 4 Shipping Production LLM systems Bridge Platform skills Backend caching, API scalability Reality check Prototype with the simplest stack that proves value; add vector databases and complex RAG only when baseline prompts and evaluation justify them.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Courses — ML and LLM application courses
  • Blogs — labs and product engineering writeups

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This path orders topics so you can grow from solid fundamentals to scalable, production-minded backend work. It mirrors how teams expect mid-level and senior engineers to reason about APIs, data, and reliability.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Backend roles span languages and frameworks, but the underlying ideas—HTTP semantics, authentication, data consistency, caching, and async work—transfer everywhere. A roadmap keeps study time aligned with interview and on-the-job expectations.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Networking & HTTP — Understand how requests move before you optimize services.
  2. Databases — Model data, query efficiently, and reason about transactions.
  3. Backend engineering — Design APIs, secure them, and structure services.
  4. System design — Compose components into reliable, scalable systems.

docs.title: Suggested sequence
docs.text: Phase Focus Start here 1 Protocols & web stack Networking introduction, HTTP and HTTPS 2 Persistence Databases introduction, SQL basics, Indexing 3 Application layer Backend introduction, REST API design, Authentication 4 Scale & ops Caching, Background jobs, API scalability 5 Architecture System design fundamentals, Distributed systems Study tip Alternate reading with small implementations: a toy REST API with auth and a database migration story beats rereading the same page.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Courses — structured programs that include backend projects
  • Books — deeper references on architecture and practice

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: System design interviews and senior IC work both reward the same skill: mapping requirements to components, trade-offs, and failure modes. This path builds from first principles to distributed patterns.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Without a sequence, it is easy to memorize diagrams without understanding why load balancers, caches, queues, or shards appear. The roadmap ties each idea to problems it solves.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Clarify requirements — Functional needs, scale, latency, and consistency expectations.
  2. High-level architecture — Clients, APIs, data stores, and async processing.
  3. Deep dives — Scaling reads and writes, messaging, and operational concerns.

docs.title: Suggested sequence
docs.text: Phase Focus Start here 1 Foundations Fundamentals, Scalability 2 Traffic & speed Load balancers, Caching 3 Data at scale Database scaling 4 Async & distribution Message queues, Distributed systems Bridge Networking context Load balancing (networking), CDN Interview pattern For every box you draw, be ready to name a failure mode (e.g., cache stampede, split brain, hot partitions) and a mitigation.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• YouTube — walkthrough-style system design channels
  • Blogs — engineering posts from large-scale systems

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Content Delivery Networks cache static (and sometimes dynamic) assets at edge locations close to users, reducing latency and origin load.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Latency and bandwidth to a single origin do not scale globally. CDNs also absorb traffic spikes and provide DDoS mitigation at the edge.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Understand caching headers, cache keys, purging/invalidation, stale-while-revalidate, TLS at edge, and dynamic acceleration patterns. Pair with HTTP caching.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   User --> Edge[Edge POP]   Edge --> Origin[Origin]   Edge -. cache hit .-> User

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Cache poisoning defense Vary cache keys on unambiguous host + path + query + negotiated representations; normalize headers used in the key.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
HTTP — cacheable response sketch 
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/javascript
Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable
ETag: "v3-abc123"

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
Why use signed URLs with CDNs?

To authorize time-limited access to private objects without exposing origin credentials to clients.

What is a cache stampede?

Many concurrent misses for a hot key hit the origin simultaneously—mitigate with request coalescing, early revalidation, or probabilistic TTL jitter.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Decide what to cache at edge for a personalized dashboard vs marketing site
  • Plan a versioned asset URL scheme for painless invalidation

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Cloudflare Learning Center — CDN
  • Fastly — caching best practices

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Domain Name System maps names to records (A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, TXT, …) through a hierarchical, delegated namespace with caching at many layers.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Humans prefer names; networks route by addresses. DNS is also a control plane for failover, traffic steering, and validation (TXT for ACME).

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Understand recursive resolvers, authoritative servers, TTL, negative caching, DNSSEC basics, and operational pain points like propagation delay and NXDOMAIN misconfigurations.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   Client --> Resolver[Recursive resolver]   Resolver --> Root[Root hints]   Resolver --> TLD[TLD servers]   Resolver --> Auth[Authoritative NS]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Caching everywhere Browser, OS stub resolver, ISP/Google/Cloudflare recursive resolver, and application libraries may cache differently—debugging requires knowing which layer answered.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
bash — query records 
dig +trace example.com
dig AAAA api.example.com @1.1.1.1

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
What is the difference between CNAME and A records?

A/AAAA point a name to addresses; CNAME aliases one name to another canonical name (with restrictions at zone apex).

Why might DNS cause intermittent failures?

Stale TTLs, partial delegation updates, resolver hijacking, or rate limiting under bursts.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Plan a blue/green DNS cutover with minimal downtime
  • Explain how split-horizon DNS works for private networks

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• DNSimple — DNS guides
  • Google — how DNS works

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: HTTP is an application protocol for resources identified by URLs, with methods, headers, and bodies. HTTPS wraps HTTP in TLS for confidentiality and integrity on the wire.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: The web runs on HTTP semantics; APIs reuse the same verbs and caching primitives. Understanding status codes, headers, and versions prevents subtle production bugs.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Study methods (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE), idempotency, status codes, caching (Cache-Control, ETag), cookies, CORS, and versions HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2 (multiplexing), HTTP/3 (QUIC).

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: sequenceDiagram   participant B as Browser   participant LB as Load balancer   participant A as API   B->>LB: GET /items (TLS)   LB->>A: Forward   A-->>B: 200 JSON

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Statelessness Each HTTP request should carry enough context (headers, cookies, tokens) for the server to handle it; sessions live in stores or tokens.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
HTTP/1.1 — raw request shapecurl — verbose TLS 
GET /v1/users/me HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.com
Authorization: Bearer <token>
Accept: application/json

curl -v https://api.example.com/v1/health

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
What is the difference between PUT and PATCH?

PUT often replaces a resource; PATCH applies partial updates—exact semantics depend on API design and idempotency expectations.

How does TLS relate to HTTPS?

TLS negotiates keys, authenticates the server (and optionally client), then encrypts HTTP messages.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Design caching headers for a versioned static asset vs personalized HTML
  • Explain CORS preflight and which requests trigger it

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• MDN — HTTP
  • RFC 9110 — HTTP semantics

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Networking describes how machines address, route, and deliver data across links and the global internet. This section builds intuition from physical links to application protocols your services depend on.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Backend engineers debug timeouts, TLS failures, DNS flaps, and load balancer behavior. Clear mental models reduce guesswork when production incidents strike.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Progress through layering (OSI model), transport (TCP and UDP), application protocols (HTTP and HTTPS), naming (DNS), traffic distribution (Load balancing), and edge delivery (CDN).

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart TB   App[Application] --> TLS[TLS]   TLS --> TCP[TCP]   TCP --> IP[IP]   IP --> Link[Link layer]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: End-to-end principle Intelligence often lives at endpoints; the network tries to deliver datagrams best-effort while higher layers add reliability and security.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
bash — inspect TLS certificate 
curl -Iv https://example.com 2>&1 | sed -n '1,20p'

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
Why is TCP considered reliable and UDP not?

TCP provides acknowledgments, retransmissions, ordering, and congestion control; UDP exposes minimal datagram delivery without those guarantees.

What problem does DNS solve?

Human-readable names to IP addresses with caching, delegation, and distributed authority.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Trace a browser request through DNS, TCP handshake, TLS, and HTTP
  • Compare latency when bypassing CDN vs edge cache hit

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Computer Networking: A Top-Down Approach — textbook
  • MDN — HTTP overview

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Load balancers distribute traffic across backends to improve utilization, availability, and scalability. They may operate at layer 4 (connections) or layer 7 (HTTP-aware routing).

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Single servers fail and saturate; load balancing spreads work and enables health checks, TLS termination, and canary routing.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Algorithms: round robin, least connections, weighted, consistent hashing for cache affinity. Features: health checks, session stickiness, TLS termination, rate limiting, WAF integration.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   Users --> LB[Load balancer]   LB --> S1[Server 1]   LB --> S2[Server 2]   LB --> S3[Server 3]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Health checks must match reality Shallow TCP checks can mark unhealthy nodes as healthy; deep checks should validate application readiness without overloading dependencies.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
YAML — conceptual k8s Service (ClusterIP vs Ingress) 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: api
spec:
  selector:
    app: api
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 8080

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
What is connection draining?

Stop sending new connections to a backend while allowing existing connections to finish before removal—important for graceful deploys.

When does consistent hashing help?

When you want minimal key remapping as nodes join/leave—common for caches and stateful partitions.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Compare L4 vs L7 load balancing for a WebSocket service
  • Design health checks for a service that depends on a flaky database

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• NGINX load balancing guide
  • AWS ELB concepts

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The OSI model partitions network communication into seven layers from physical bits to application semantics. Modern stacks often teach the TCP/IP model, but OSI terminology still appears in interviews and documentation.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Layered models separate concerns: cabling vs framing vs routing vs sessions vs applications. They help localize failures (“is it layer 4 or layer 7?”).

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Layers (bottom-up): Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application. Map real protocols: Ethernet/Wi-Fi (1–2), IP (3), TCP/UDP (4), TLS (5–6-ish), HTTP (7).

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart TB   L7[Application HTTP]   L6[Presentation TLS payload]   L5[Session]   L4[Transport TCP]   L3[Network IP]   L2[Data Link Ethernet]   L1[Physical]   L7 --> L4   L4 --> L3   L3 --> L2

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Not a law of physics Real systems blur boundaries—TLS spans what OSI would split across layers; use the model to communicate, not to force reality into boxes.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Text — mapping a HTTPS call 
HTTP message -> TLS record -> TCP segment -> IP packet -> Ethernet frame

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
Where does a router operate vs a switch?

Routers forward based on IP (layer 3); switches typically forward frames by MAC (layer 2); modern devices blur lines with L3 switches.

Why do people say load balancers can be L4 or L7?

L4 balances TCP/UDP flows with little protocol awareness; L7 understands HTTP headers, cookies, and routing rules.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Label each hop in a traceroute with the closest OSI layer
  • Explain where fragmentation and reassembly occur in IP vs TCP

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Cloudflare — OSI model
  • RFC 1122 — host requirements — practical layering discussion

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: TCP provides reliable, ordered byte streams with congestion control. UDP provides connectionless datagrams with minimal overhead—ideal for latency-sensitive or broadcast-style workloads where the app handles loss.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Different applications need different trade-offs between latency, throughput, ordering, and overhead. Transport protocols encode those trade-offs.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: TCP: three-way handshake, sequence numbers, ACKs, retransmissions, flow control (windowing), congestion control (Cubic, BBR). UDP: send and forget; optionally pair with app-level reliability (QUIC builds on UDP with modern features).

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: sequenceDiagram   participant C as Client   participant S as Server   C->>S: SYN   S->>C: SYN-ACK   C->>S: ACK

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Head-of-line blocking TCP’s ordered delivery can delay later bytes if an early segment is lost—HTTP/3/QUIC mitigates at application scope with separate streams.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Python — TCP echo (conceptual) 
import socket

def run_echo_server(host: str = "127.0.0.1", port: int = 9000) -> None:
    with socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM) as s:
        s.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, socket.SO_REUSEADDR, 1)
        s.bind((host, port))
        s.listen()
        conn, _ = s.accept()
        with conn:
            data = conn.recv(4096)
            conn.sendall(data)

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
When would you choose UDP?

Real-time media with app-tolerated loss, DNS (traditionally), gaming, or when building custom reliability like QUIC.

What is TIME_WAIT in TCP?

A state ensuring stray segments drain after close; can exhaust ephemeral ports under churn—tune kernel settings carefully on high-QPS clients.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Compare behavior under packet loss for a TCP download vs a UDP video stream
  • Explain how BBR differs from loss-based congestion control at a high level

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• High Performance Browser Networking — TCP
  • RFC 9293 — TCP

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Engineering blogs publish postmortems, performance deep dives, and architecture retrospectives from production systems. They complement textbooks with current practice.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: You learn what teams actually ship, including mistakes and metrics rarely found in courses.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Subscribe to a small set, read weekly, and file away patterns (caching incidents, DB migrations, rollout strategies).

  • Infra & cloud: AWS, GCP, Azure architecture blogs
  • Databases: PostgreSQL planet, Cockroach Labs, PlanetScale
  • AI: Google AI, OpenAI Research, Anthropic research

Independently operated

Listings are for education; no affiliation or endorsement implied.

docs.text: Prefer primary sources Summaries help, but reading the original post preserves nuance and numbers.

Title:
Claim:
Evidence (metrics):

docs.title: Interview Questions

docs.text: This page lists widely recommended books across the Engineering OS curriculum. Use it as a starting library—not an exhaustive catalog.

docs.text: Read actively Summarize each chapter in your own words and link to one real system you have used.

Skim chapter -> implement one exercise -> write 5 bullet notes -> add to flashcards

docs.text: Projects over certificates Hiring loops value artifacts you can discuss; prioritize courses that produce code or designs you own.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: YouTube channels provide visual explanations for algorithms, system design, and tooling. Use them to complement reading and implementation—not replace it.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Complex flows (Kafka, DNS, GPU kernels) are easier to grasp with diagrams and animations.

  • System design: whiteboard-style sessions—compare multiple channels for consensus
  • Infrastructure: cloud provider channels for service deep dives

docs.text: Verify claims Prefer channels that cite sources and show trade-offs; avoid hype-only content.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Text — active viewing 
Pause every 5 minutes -> summarize aloud -> note one question to test with a doc/source

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
Are YouTube system design videos enough?

They help for narration practice; you still need to draw diagrams yourself and simulate follow-up questions.

  • Implement a tiny component mentioned (e.g., rate limiter) over a weekend

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: At scale, caching is often the difference between a responsive system and a database bottleneck. System design asks where caches live, how they invalidate, and what consistency users observe.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Repeated reads dominate many workloads; moving hot data closer to users reduces latency and cost.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Layers: browser, CDN, reverse proxy, application, distributed cache, database buffer pool. Patterns: cache-aside, read/write-through, write-behind. Pitfalls: staleness, thundering herds, invalidation storms.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   Client --> CDN[CDN]   CDN --> App[App]   App --> Redis[(Redis cluster)]   App --> DB[(DB)]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Consistency contract Document whether users can read stale data and for how long; drive TTL and invalidation from product requirements.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Text — invalidation strategy 
- Time-based TTL for low-risk data
- Event-driven invalidation on writes for critical entities
- Versioned keys for immutable snapshots

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
How do CDNs interact with authenticated APIs?

Usually only public assets cache at edge; private APIs use short TTLs, signed URLs, or no CDN caching—validate per route.

What is a cache coherency problem?

Multiple caches hold inconsistent copies of the same entity—mitigate with TTL, pub/sub invalidation, or single writer paths.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Cache a user profile that updates frequently vs a static catalog
  • Design a leaderboard with Redis sorted sets and periodic snapshots

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Caching at scale (Facebook/Meta engineering talks)
  • Redis persistence and replication

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Database scaling tackles read/write growth beyond single-node limits through replication, partitioning, specialized engines, and application-level patterns.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: The database is often the hardest component to scale; its data model and consistency constraints ripple through the whole architecture.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Read scaling: replicas, caching, CQRS. Write scaling: sharding, partitioning keys, avoiding hot partitions. Operational: backups, failover, online schema changes. Align with Databases — scaling.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   Router[Shard router] --> S1[(Shard 1)]   Router --> S2[(Shard 2)]   S1 --> R1[Replica]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Choose the shard key wisely Skewed keys undo horizontal scaling; measure distribution and rebalance with operational tooling.

docs.title: Code Examples
Interview Questions
docs.text:
What is the difference between replication and sharding?

Replication copies the same dataset for availability and read scale; sharding splits different subsets of data across nodes.

How does two-phase commit relate to scaling?

2PC coordinates distributed transactions but increases latency and failure modes—often avoided in favor of sagas and idempotent steps.

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
• Shard a multi-tenant messaging system by conversation id
  • Evaluate CQRS for a read-heavy analytics dashboard

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Designing Data-Intensive Applications
  • Google Spanner paper — global SQL at scale

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Distributed systems coordinate independent nodes over unreliable networks. Core concepts include clocks, failure models, consensus, replication, and consistency trade-offs.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Large-scale systems are always distributed; understanding limitations prevents impossible designs and explains real-world behavior.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Topics: CAP as a teaching lens (not a literal binary), linearizability, serializability, eventual consistency, vector clocks, leader election, Raft/Paxos at high level, idempotency, exactly-once semantics (end-to-end), byzantine vs crash faults.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: graph TB   subgraph Cluster     L[Leader]     F1[Follower]     F2[Follower]   end   L --> F1   L --> F2

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Networks partition Assume messages can be delayed, duplicated, or dropped; design APIs and storage operations to be safe under retries.

PUT /items/{id} with full state is naturally idempotent
POST /charges with Idempotency-Key header dedupes on server

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
What is the two generals problem?

Impossibility of guaranteed agreement over unreliable communication—motivates why protocols use timeouts and probabilistic guarantees.

Explain split-brain and mitigation.

Multiple nodes believe they are primary; mitigate with quorum (majority), fencing tokens, and careful failover automation.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: System design starts from requirements: functional features, scale, latency, consistency, durability, and cost. You propose components and data flows, then iterate on bottlenecks and failure modes.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Engineering organizations need shared vocabulary for trade-offs: replication vs partitioning, sync vs async, strong vs eventual consistency.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Clarify users and QPS, read/write ratio, data size and growth, SLA/SLO, and compliance. Sketch APIs, data model, storage, caching, load balancing, async processing, and observability.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: graph LR   Client --> LB[Load Balancer]   LB --> API[Stateless API]   API --> DB[(Primary DB)]   API --> Cache[(Cache)]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Start simple, justify complexity Reach for a queue, shard, or multi-region setup only when the problem and scale demand it—avoid resume-driven architecture.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Text — requirement checklist 
- Who are the users? mobile/web/internal
- Peak QPS and payload sizes
- Consistency: can reads lag? by how long?
- Durability: RPO/RTO targets
- Multi-tenant vs single-tenant isolation

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
How do you begin a system design interview?

Ask clarifying questions, define scope, enumerate core entities and operations, estimate capacity, then propose a high-level design before deep dives.

What is the difference between latency and throughput?

Latency is time to service a request; throughput is completed work per unit time—optimizing one can hurt the other without careful tuning.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Load balancers distribute traffic across healthy instances to balance utilization and improve availability. They can terminate TLS, enforce routing rules, and shield origins from overload.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Single points of failure and saturation disappear when requests are spread across pools with health-aware routing.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Algorithms and features mirror Networking — load balancing, but system design focuses on session affinity, blue/green deployments, canary routing, and global vs regional load balancing.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   G[Global LB] --> R1[Region 1]   G --> R2[Region 2]   R1 --> LB[Regional LB]   LB --> Pod[Pods]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Sticky sessions Pinning users can break even load during deploys; prefer stateless services with shared session stores when possible.

        paths:

            pathType: Prefix

                name: api
                port:

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
How do you drain connections during deploy?

Remove instance from rotation, wait for active connections to finish or timeout, then deploy—coordinate with health checks.

What is anycast?

Same IP served from multiple locations; routing uses BGP to reach nearest POP—used by global load balancing and CDNs.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Message queues buffer work between producers and consumers, enabling asynchronous processing, load leveling, and decoupled services.

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Synchronous chains amplify tail latency and failures. Queues absorb spikes and provide a retry surface for transient errors.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Compare queues (SQS, RabbitMQ) vs logs/streams (Kafka, Pulsar). Concepts: ordering, delivery semantics, dead-letter queues, consumer groups, backpressure, schema evolution for events.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart LR   A[Service A] --> K[Kafka topic]   K --> C1[Consumer group]   K --> C2[Consumer group]

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Ordering guarantees Global ordering is expensive; partition by entity id to preserve ordering per key while parallelizing across partitions.

docs.title: Code Examples
Interview Questions
docs.text:
When would you pick Kafka over RabbitMQ?

Kafka for high-throughput event logs, replay, and stream processing; RabbitMQ for flexible routing and task queues—depends on workload.

What is consumer lag?

The difference between produced and processed offsets—monitor SLOs on lag to detect slow consumers.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Scalability is the ability to handle growth—more users, data, and regions—without proportional loss of performance or reliability. It spans vertical (bigger machines) and horizontal (more machines).

docs.title: Why This Exists
docs.text: Products rarely fail from lack of features alone; they fail from outages, cost explosions, or latency regressions under load.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Dimensions: scale up/out, partitioning, replication, caching, stateless tiers, rate limiting, autoscaling, multi-region strategies. Watch hotspots and coordination overhead.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: flowchart TB   subgraph Region A     LB1[LB] --> AP1[API]     AP1 --> DB1[(DB)]   end   subgraph Region B     LB2[LB] --> AP2[API]     AP2 --> DB2[(DB)]   end

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Utilization targets Run production with headroom; auto-scaling should react before SLO breaches—validate with load tests and chaos experiments.

docs.title: Code Examples
docs.text:
Text — back-of-envelope 
1M DAU, each 20 requests/day -> ~230 RPS average
Peak ~10x average -> ~2.3k RPS; size instances accordingly

docs.title: Interview Questions
docs.text:
What is the difference between strong and eventual consistency?

Strong consistency guarantees reads reflect latest writes; eventual consistency allows temporary divergence across replicas.

Name a coordination service bottleneck.

A single global counter or lock can serialize traffic—shard or approximate (e.g., HLL for counts).
